executive_summary:
After analyzing 16 prior art systems spanning 45 years of online community governance, a clear pattern emerges: successful decentralized moderation requires three mutually reinforcing mechanisms—friction (cost to act), decay (organic content expiration), and exit (fork capability). No single mechanism suffices. Usenet's pure decentralization failed without coordination; Reddit's voting is trivially gamed without friction; Wikipedia's consensus works only with ultimate authority (ArbCom). Swimchain's proposed combination of PoW friction, engagement-based decay, and fork-as-exit represents the most promising synthesis, directly validated by the Hive/Steem fork case.

The research reveals that Swimchain's intuitions are well-founded but require specific implementation choices. Reputation should use labels not numbers (Slashdot lesson). Shared filter lists need 'receipts' documenting reasons (Mastodon lesson). Node-level filtering can scale to billions of messages daily (email lesson). Most importantly, persistent pseudonymous identity—not anonymity—is essential; 4chan's failure mode demonstrates that anonymity without reputation consequences enables worst-case outcomes.

The primary gap is coordinated harassment, which no decentralized system has solved. Secondary gaps include AI-generated content at scale, Sybil resistance without identity verification, and protecting minorities within communities who cannot meaningfully fork. These should inform Swimchain's v1 scope decisions—some problems may be explicitly deferred rather than poorly solved.

approach_categories.general_tradeoffs: Maximum decentralization preserved—no single entity can force network-wide decisions. However, requires coordination mechanisms (shared lists, reputation signals) to scale beyond individual judgment. Works best when nodes can make independent decisions using shared signals. Email proves this scales to billions of daily messages. Weakness: coordination is voluntary, so bad actors can find permissive nodes.

Creates skin-in-the-game and distributes moderation labor to trusted community members. Reduces new-user friction while protecting against Sybil attacks. All systems show gaming occurs—question is whether gaming undermines core function. Exposing exact numbers encourages gaming; labels work better. Requires some central authority to define thresholds at protocol level.

Intuitive and scalable, but highly susceptible to manipulation. Reddit demonstrates that without friction, voting is trivially gamed (documented campaigns achieving 5.7M upvotes in 15 days). Stake-weighted systems become plutocratic. Anonymous voting enables brigading. Works better when combined with friction (PoW) and reputation weighting.

Clear accountability and effective at scale when trust is established. Works for self-selected, aligned communities (HN, Wikipedia). Vulnerable to moderator capture, burnout, and single points of failure. Reddit's 2023 API crisis showed volunteer moderators have no leverage when platform doesn't depend on them. Requires users to trust governance hierarchy.

Shifts moderation from active removal to passive expiration. Unpopular/harmful content fades without explicit decisions. Enables different expression patterns (people post differently knowing content will disappear). Pure temporal decay (4chan) loses valuable content; engagement-based decay (Swimchain) creates community ownership of what persists. Gaming possible through fake engagement.

The ultimate governance escape—when capture occurs, community can leave with preserved identity. Hive proves this works in practice (2x more daily users post-fork). Requires identity portability and content history preservation. Creates network effect fragmentation. Not suitable for every disagreement—some coordination required. The credible threat of fork disciplines governance without requiring governance mechanism.

Theoretical best-of-both-worlds: anonymity with accountability. Limited real-world deployment in social media context. Complexity is high. 2024 research on decentralized issuance (Blind Multisignatures) removes some centralization concerns. Worth watching for Swimchain v2 but too complex for v1.

approaches.name: Federated Node Filtering (Email model)
approaches.notes: Proven at internet scale. Each node decides independently using shared signals. Swimchain's natural fit.

approaches.name: Mastodon Consensus Blocklists
approaches.notes: Multi-source requirement reduces false positives. Transparency with 'receipts' builds trust.

approaches.name: Stack Overflow Graduated Privileges
approaches.notes: Works at client level for Swimchain. Cannot be protocol-level without central threshold authority.

approaches.name: PoW Friction (Swimchain)
approaches.notes: Novel in social media context. Email hashcash never achieved adoption. Accessibility concerns.

approaches.name: Engagement-Based Decay (Swimchain)
approaches.notes: Novel mechanism. Gaming through fake engagement possible. Needs prototype testing.

approaches.name: Fork-as-Exit (Hive model)
approaches.notes: Proven by Hive case study. Requires identity portability (Swimchain has this). Community coordination needed.

approaches.name: Reddit Voting
approaches.notes: Trivially gamed without friction. Swimchain's PoW would help but voting still problematic.

approaches.name: Steemit Stake-Weighted Voting
approaches.notes: Creates plutocracy. Directly contradicts Swimchain's anti-concentration values.

approaches.name: Blind Signatures/zk-Reputation
approaches.notes: Promising but not battle-tested. Consider for Swimchain v2.

chainsocial_incompatible.reason: Directly creates plutocracy. Token holders dominate regardless of contribution quality. Violates Swimchain's core value of 'no mega-nodes, no central authority.' Steemit demonstrated that stake-weighted systems favor accumulators over creators. Swimchain explicitly rejects any mechanism where power accumulates to holders.

Creates single points of capture and failure. Violates 'no central authority' and 'forks over consensus.' Even benevolent hierarchies (HN's dang) are fundamentally dependent on trust in central parties. Swimchain's protocol cannot have moderator roles—this is a core thesis.

Requires final authority (ArbCom) to resolve irreconcilable disputes. Swimchain's answer to irreconcilable disputes is forking, not arbitration. Wikipedia's 'never-ending wars' category validates that some disputes cannot be resolved through consensus—Swimchain acknowledges this by enabling exit.

Requires central control over ranking/visibility. HN's success depends entirely on dang's judgment and community trust in single moderator. This is incompatible with Swimchain's decentralization—no single entity should control content visibility network-wide.

chainsocial_incompatible.approach: Anonymous posting without persistent identity (4chan model)
chainsocial_incompatible.reason: Enables worst-case outcomes when combined with minimal moderation. 4chan's 11% hate speech rate and links to real-world violence demonstrate the failure mode. Swimchain requires persistent pseudonymous identity so reputation has consequences. The 'pseudonymity, not anonymity' distinction is critical.

Violates user autonomy and creates capture vector. Even well-intentioned central blocklists can be weaponized. Swimchain's approach is client-side filtering with user control over which lists to subscribe to. Users must always have the ability to override any shared list.

primary.approach: Layered Defense: PoW Friction + Engagement-Based Decay + Node-Level Filtering + Fork Capability
primary.rationale:
No single mechanism works in isolation—the research clearly shows this. Swimchain should implement mutually reinforcing layers:

1. **PoW Friction**: Every action has computational cost, making spam economically irrational. Unlike voting systems, this cannot be cheaply gamed—you cannot buy PoW solutions, only compute them. This addresses Reddit/Steemit's core vulnerability.

2. **Engagement-Based Decay**: Content without ongoing engagement fades organically. This provides moderation without moderators—unpopular or harmful content disappears without explicit removal decisions. Unlike 4chan's temporal decay, community engagement determines what persists.

3. **Node-Level Filtering**: Each node makes independent filtering decisions using shared signals (reputation, shared blocklists). This mirrors email's proven architecture—billions of messages filtered daily without central authority. Nodes can adopt shared lists but retain ultimate control.

4. **Fork Capability**: When governance fails at any level, communities can exit with preserved identity and content. Hive's success proves this works. The credible threat of fork disciplines all other mechanisms without requiring governance.

This combination addresses the key failure modes:
- PoW prevents cheap manipulation (Reddit's vulnerability)
- Decay prevents stale harmful content (unlike permanent archives)
- Node filtering prevents mega-node capture (unlike centralized platforms)
- Fork prevents community capture (unlike Reddit's moderator trap)
tradeoffs_accepted: PoW creates accessibility barriers for users with limited compute—mitigated by adaptive difficulty and potential for light-client solutions
Decay may lose valuable niche content that would be recognized later—accepted as cost of organic moderation
Node filtering creates inconsistent network views—accepted as feature, not bug; each community chooses its reality
Fork requires coordination and creates fragmentation—accepted as price of exit rights; some fragmentation is healthy
No single source of truth about what content 'should' exist—philosophical shift from centralized platforms
open_questions: What PoW difficulty level balances spam prevention with accessibility?
How quickly should content decay without engagement? Logarithmic? Exponential?
How do nodes discover and evaluate shared filter lists without central registry?
What mechanisms help communities coordinate forks when needed?
How to handle decay gaming through fake engagement rings?

alternatives.when_to_use: When the protocol-level mechanisms prove insufficient and communities need finer-grained control. Clients can implement Stack Overflow-style reputation thresholds locally—low-reputation content collapsed by default, high-reputation content prioritized.
alternatives.tradeoffs: Inconsistent experience across clients. Creates implicit hierarchy. Reputation cold-start problem for new users. But preserves protocol simplicity while enabling sophisticated filtering.

alternatives.when_to_use: When shared filter lists emerge and need trust mechanisms. Require 2+ independent sources to agree before auto-adoption. All blocks must include documented reasons ('receipts'). Mastodon's evolution shows this builds trust.
alternatives.tradeoffs: Slower response to emerging threats. Coordination overhead. But reduces false positives and weaponization of blocklists.

alternatives.when_to_use: When different communities need different moderation policies. Spaces set parameters at creation (decay rate, PoW difficulty, engagement thresholds) that are immutable. Users self-select into spaces matching their preferences.
alternatives.tradeoffs: Complexity in space discovery. Users may not understand parameter implications. But enables experimentation without protocol changes.

alternatives.when_to_use: When Sybil attacks become a problem. Persistent keypairs accumulate reputation from node observations and community interactions. New identities start with no reputation; established identities have track records.
alternatives.tradeoffs: Privacy implications of tracking identity behavior. Reputation may not transfer across contexts. Cold-start problem. But provides Sybil resistance without identity verification.

rejected.approach: Any form of voting on content visibility
rejected.reason: Reddit's experience proves voting is trivially gamed without significant friction. Even with PoW, voting creates manipulation incentives. Swimchain's decay mechanism provides similar signal (what people engage with persists) without gameable voting scores.

Steemit conclusively demonstrated that stake-weighted systems create plutocracy. Any mechanism where power accumulates to token/stake holders will be captured by accumulators. Swimchain's PoW is deliberately non-accumulating—you must recompute for each action.

Central to Swimchain's thesis. Moderators create capture points and single points of failure. All 'moderation' must emerge from PoW friction, decay, node filtering, or fork—never from privileged actors at protocol level.

There is no objective truth about what content 'should' exist. Different communities have different standards. Protocol should enable diverse realities through node-level filtering, not enforce single reality through consensus.

While Slashdot's experience shows that exposing exact karma numbers encourages gaming, completely hiding reputation prevents informed decisions. Recommendation: use labels ('established', 'new', 'flagged') rather than exact numbers, similar to Slashdot's evolution.

Violates Swimchain's pseudonymity principle. Identity verification requires central authority and destroys privacy. PoW provides Sybil resistance without identity verification—each action costs regardless of how many identities you control.

dependencies: SYNTHESIS_01_IDENTITY: Persistent keypairs enable reputation accumulation and fork portability
SYNTHESIS_02_POW: PoW mechanism must be defined before moderation patterns can leverage it
SYNTHESIS_03_CONTENT_DISTRIBUTION: Node filtering depends on content propagation model
Space specification: Space parameters affect decay rates and moderation context

complexity_breakdown.pow_friction: medium - PoW algorithm is straightforward; difficulty calibration requires testing
complexity_breakdown.engagement_decay: medium - decay formula is simple; edge cases (spam engagement, decay gaming) need handling
complexity_breakdown.node_filtering: low - each node implements locally; shared list format needs specification
complexity_breakdown.fork_capability: high - identity portability exists but content migration and community coordination need design
complexity_breakdown.reputation_accumulation: high - what behaviors affect reputation? how do nodes share reputation signals? privacy implications?

prototype_questions: What PoW difficulty makes spam irrational while remaining accessible on commodity hardware?
How quickly does content decay feel right? Test with simulated communities.
Do users understand engagement-based decay intuitively, or does content 'disappearing' feel broken?
How do shared filter lists propagate without becoming centralized? Test discovery mechanisms.
Can decay be gamed with engagement rings? How detectable is this?
What's the minimum viable fork? Can a single user fork, or only communities?
How do new users bootstrap without reputation? Is the cold-start problem acceptable?
Does PoW create significant accessibility barriers on mobile devices? Low-powered hardware?

components: Local node filtering (no shared lists yet)
implementation_phases.validation: Can users post? Does spam become costly? Does content decay? Can users switch nodes?
Can nodes coordinate filtering without central authority? Do consensus lists reduce false positives?
Can a community successfully fork? Do users follow? Does fork discipline governance?

remaining_gaps.description: No decentralized system has solved targeted, multi-account harassment. Attackers can follow victims across migrations, create new identities faster than victims can block, and coordinate across platforms. PoW slows but doesn't prevent determined attackers.
potential_mitigations: High reputation thresholds for interacting with established users
Client-side tools for detecting coordination patterns
Community-level blocking (entire spaces block harasser networks)
Consider: identity staking where harassment results in reputational cost
remaining_gaps.recommendation: Acknowledge this gap explicitly. Design for harm reduction, not prevention. Enable victims to quickly block interaction patterns, not just individual identities.

remaining_gaps.description: All decentralized systems struggle with CSAM and similar content. Node-level filtering removes visibility but content technically exists on some nodes. Legal exposure for node operators is unclear.
potential_mitigations: PhotoDNA-style hashing for known illegal content (privacy tradeoff)
Node operators can choose filtering aggressiveness
Consider: content not propagated until PoW + time threshold (delays distribution)
Legal framing: nodes are carriers, not publishers (email model)
remaining_gaps.recommendation: Consult legal experts. Consider PhotoDNA integration for known CSAM. Be explicit that nodes have filtering responsibility. This may limit node operator population to those willing to moderate.

remaining_gaps.description: 2024-2025 research shows AI-generated spam/manipulation is accelerating. Traditional moderation approaches struggle. 4chan research found 69.7% of AI-generated images contained recognizable figures in extremist content.
potential_mitigations: Higher PoW difficulty for image/media content
Client-side AI detection (imperfect but helpful)
Reputation weighting penalizes new accounts with high-volume posting
Consider: content provenance/signing for human verification
remaining_gaps.recommendation: PoW helps more than traditional systems since AI generation is cheap but PoW is not. Monitor AI detection research. Consider content provenance in future versions.

remaining_gaps.description: Fork/exit rights protect communities from platform capture but not dissidents within communities. A lone disagreer cannot meaningfully fork—they lose their community. Majority can effectively silence minority views.
potential_mitigations: Low barriers to space creation (easy to start alternative)
Cross-space identity reputation persists
Client-side tools for finding like-minded users across spaces
Consider: minority fork mechanism with lower threshold
remaining_gaps.recommendation: Acknowledge that Swimchain optimizes for community exit, not individual protection within communities. Individuals can leave for different spaces. Some communities will be hostile—this is acceptable given easy exit.

remaining_gaps.description: All systems relying on volunteer moderation face burnout. Even distributed moderation has labor costs. Wikipedia, Reddit, and forum moderators consistently report burnout.
potential_mitigations: PoW + decay reduce active moderation needed
Node filtering is automated once configured
No moderator roles means no moderator burnout (by design)
Community rotation of filter list maintenance
remaining_gaps.recommendation: Swimchain's design explicitly minimizes moderation labor through automation (PoW, decay). Remaining labor (filter list maintenance) should be distributed. This is a feature, not a gap to solve.

remaining_gaps.description: Content that becomes valuable later may decay before value is recognized. Niche content without immediate engagement could be lost.
potential_mitigations: Personal archives (users save their own content)
Client-side 'pin' that prevents decay for user's own content
Spaces with different decay parameters for archival purposes
Consider: 'revival' mechanism where old content can be re-engaged
remaining_gaps.recommendation: Accept some content loss as cost of organic moderation. Users who value content can preserve it locally. Archival spaces can set slow decay. Not every tweet needs to be permanent.

key_lessons_from_prior_art.application: Swimchain's fork capability is not just escape hatch—it's the primary governance mechanism. The credible threat of exit disciplines all other systems.

key_lessons_from_prior_art.lesson: Completely decentralized moderation requires coordination layer
key_lessons_from_prior_art.application: Pure decentralization without shared signals eventually degrades. Swimchain needs shared blocklist mechanisms even though adoption is voluntary.

key_lessons_from_prior_art.lesson: Reputation systems are always gamed—design for it
key_lessons_from_prior_art.application: Don't expose exact numbers. Use labels. Accept some gaming as cost of distributed trust. Design so gaming undermines itself.

key_lessons_from_prior_art.lesson: Federated filtering scales to internet scale
key_lessons_from_prior_art.application: The email model proves that decentralized reputation and filtering works at scale. Swimchain's architecture mirrors this proven pattern.

key_lessons_from_prior_art.lesson: Transparency builds trust in shared lists
key_lessons_from_prior_art.application: Blocklists with documented reasons ('receipts') are more trusted. Swimchain filter lists should require documentation for entries.
Swimchain's persistent pseudonymity is not optional—it's essential. Reputation must have consequences. The distinction from anonymity is critical.
Temporal limits change expression patterns and provide organic content management. Swimchain's engagement-based decay is evolution of this pattern.
When users choose to join communities with aligned values, moderation needs decrease. Swimchain's space model assumes self-selection.
Single-factor reputation is easier to game. Swimchain should combine PoW history, engagement patterns, node observations, and community signals.
When every action has cost, spam becomes irrational. Unlike voting, PoW cannot be cheaply bought or coordinated. Core to Swimchain's anti-manipulation strategy.

high_confidence: Layered defense (PoW + decay + filtering + fork) is the right architecture
Stake-weighted mechanisms will create plutocracy and should be rejected
Protocol-level moderator roles violate core thesis and should not exist
Persistent pseudonymous identity is essential (not anonymity)
Fork capability is primary governance mechanism
Node-level filtering can scale (email proves this)
medium_confidence: PoW difficulty can be calibrated for accessibility while preventing spam
Engagement-based decay will feel intuitive to users
Shared blocklists with receipts will emerge and be trusted
Reputation labels (vs numbers) will reduce gaming incentives
low_confidence: Coordinated harassment can be adequately addressed
AI-generated content can be detected/managed at scale
Minority protection within hostile communities is achievable
Exact decay formula that balances preservation and organic moderation
requires_testing: PoW difficulty calibration across device types
Decay rate that feels 'right' to users
